qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Expired proxy-auth JWTs remain valid and renewable on protected routes when custom logout is enabled
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The finding targets expired proxy-auth JWT acceptance in the `withUser` and `/api/renew` flow when proxy auth and a custom logout page are enabled. The advisory targets a different root cause: unauthenticated `POST /api/signup` creates new users with `defaults.perm.admin=true` because `signupHandler` applies `settings.Defaults` to the new user without stripping admin privileges.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No final qualifying finding identifies the advisory-described signup-to-admin root cause. The only qualifying finding is a separate proxy-auth expired-token acceptance bug in `http/auth.go` and `/api/renew`, not the advisory's `signupHandler` plus `settings.Defaults.Apply` privilege-assignment flaw.
evidence: The advisory describes an unauthenticated `POST /api/signup` path where `signupHandler` applies `d.settings.Defaults` to a new user and can preserve `perm.admin=true`, granting administrator privileges to self-registered accounts.

The only qualifying finding report describes a different attack path: an attacker reuses an already issued low-privilege proxy-auth JWT after expiry, and the vulnerable operations are the proxy-auth expiry check and token renewal flow.

The session manifest lists only `/Users/lumi/ziyue/EvoHunt-Run/advisory_da38f58a/proxy_auth_expired_token_reuse` as a final `qualifying_vuln` finding, so there is no qualifying finding covering the advisory's signup/default-permissions code path.
